title: Pure and module tests

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific JavaScript: Language and Browser Applications draft for Pure and module tests; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: MDN JavaScript Guide; accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Pure and module tests
sources.claim: Unit tests verify deterministic module contracts with controlled inputs and dependencies, including boundaries and failures.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Unit tests verify deterministic module contracts with controlled inputs and dependencies, including boundaries and failures.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A price function is tested at zero, threshold, normal, and invalid input without a browser. Your goal is to create tests covering normal, boundary and failure behavior for non-DOM modules. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Browser state and security; Dependency boundaries; Errors, throw and recovery boundaries. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: Unit tests verify deterministic module contracts with controlled inputs and dependencies, including boundaries and failures. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Overmocking tests implementation, timers and randomness need control, and coverage is not correctness. This draft therefore rejects: One happy-path assertion is enough when line coverage reaches 100 percent.

includes: Test deterministic domain behavior and module contracts with isolated fixtures.
Create tests covering normal, boundary and failure behavior for non-DOM modules.
excludes: Overmocking tests implementation, timers and randomness need control, and coverage is not correctness.

outcomes: Create tests covering normal, boundary and failure behavior for non-DOM modules.

examples.title: Pure and module tests: concrete case
examples.situation: A price function is tested at zero, threshold, normal, and invalid input without a browser.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A price function is tested at zero, threshold, normal, and invalid input without a browser.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Derive tests from contract partitions and boundaries; coverage alone does not establish behavior.
examples.result: The result follows because Derive tests from contract partitions and boundaries; coverage alone does not establish behavior.

examples.title: Pure and module tests: boundary case
examples.situation: Overmocking tests implementation, timers and randomness need control, and coverage is not correctness.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Start from the documented boundary: Overmocking tests implementation, timers and randomness need control, and coverage is not correctness.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: One happy-path assertion is enough when line coverage reaches 100 percent.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: Derive tests from contract partitions and boundaries; coverage alone does not establish behavior.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: One happy-path assertion is enough when line coverage reaches 100 percent.
misconceptions.correction: Derive tests from contract partitions and boundaries; coverage alone does not establish behavior.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use a concrete JavaScript: Language and Browser Applications artifact to explain why this belief fails: One happy-path assertion is enough when line coverage reaches 100 percent.

analogy.example: A coordinated event desk can model Pure and module tests, with values, tasks, callbacks, and external messages changing observable state.
usefulFor: Building a first mental model for Pure and module tests before using exact platform terms
limitations: Human coordination does not reproduce coercion, lexical scope, microtasks, prototypes, or browser security boundaries.
Overmocking tests implementation, timers and randomness need control, and coverage is not correctness.

recap.summary: You should now be able to create tests covering normal, boundary and failure behavior for non-DOM modules. Keep this boundary in view: Overmocking tests implementation, timers and randomness need control, and coverage is not correctness.

Why is this claim incorrect: One happy-path assertion is enough when line coverage reaches 100 percent.
Apply checkpoint 'contract partitions' to a new artifact.
recap.nextReviewPrompt: Next: continue to “DOM and asynchronous integration tests”. Before moving on, answer one retrieval prompt without reopening the explanation.
